subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Accumulation Function from Piecewise Graph (FTC, Absolute Min)
stimulus: The continuous function $f$ is defined on the closed interval $-6 \le x \le 12$. The graph of $f$, consisting of two semicircles and one line segment, is shown in the figure. Let $g$ be the function defined by $g(x) = \int_6^x f(t)\, dt$.

parts.prompt: Find $g'(8)$. Give a reason for your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: P1 earned for $g'(x) = f(x)$ or $g'(8) = f(8)$. P2 earned for $g'(8) = 1$ consistent with the graph value $f(8) = 1$. A response of $g'(8) = f(8) - f(6)$ earns P2 but not P1.

scoring_points.description: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, $g'(x) = f(x)$

required_elements: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, $g'(x) = f(x)$

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: states $g'(x) = f(x)$ or $g'(8) = f(8)$ via FTC.

sample_responses.response: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, $g'(x) = f(x)$, so $g'(8) = f(8) = 1$.
sample_responses.commentary: FTC Part 1: derivative of accumulation function equals the integrand evaluated at $x$.
This earns P2 (correct value) but not P1; FTC gives $g'(x)=f(x)$ directly, not $f(x)-f(6)$.
Incorrect graph reading; $f(8)=1$ on the linear portion of the graph.

parts.prompt: Find all values of $x$ in the open interval $-6 < x < 12$ at which the graph of $g$ has a point of inflection. Give a reason for your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: P3 requires all three values $x = -3, 3, 6$ and no additional values. P4 requires tying the reason to changes in $f$ (not just saying '$g$ changes concavity'). A response with two of three correct values and correct reasoning earns P4 but not P3.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: exactly $x=-3$, $x=3$, and $x=6$ — no additional values and none missing.

sample_responses.commentary: All three values where $f$ attains a relative extremum (and thus $f'$ changes sign); no extras.
Including $x=9$ as an additional point of inflection is incorrect; $f$ does not change between increasing/decreasing at $x=9$, so P3 is not earned.

scoring_points.description: Correct reason tied to the graph of $f$

required_elements: Correct reason tied to the graph of $f$
alternatives.correct_example: $g$ has points of inflection where $g'' = f'$ changes sign, i.e., where $f$ changes from increasing to decreasing or decreasing to increasing

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: reason tied to $f$ — $g$ has inflection points where $f$ changes from increasing to decreasing or vice versa (i.e., where $f$ has a relative extremum).

sample_responses.response: $g$ has points of inflection at $x=-3$, $x=3$, $x=6$ because $f$ (equivalently $g'$) changes from decreasing to increasing or increasing to decreasing at those $x$-values.
sample_responses.commentary: Ties the reason to the behavior of $f$, as required by the rubric.

sample_responses.response: $g$ has points of inflection at those $x$-values because $g$ changes concavity there.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses an ambiguous term 'the graph' or states concavity without tying it to $f$; rubric requires the reason to reference $f$ (the derivative of $g$).

parts.scoring_notes: Labels are required — unlabeled values earn no credit. $g(12) = 9$ (area of triangle from $x=6$ to $x=12$). $g(0) = -\frac{9\pi}{2}$ (negative because integrating backwards over a region where $f \ge 0$, using the upper semicircle of radius 3). Accept exact forms.

scoring_points.description: $g(12) = \int_6^{12} f(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{2}(6)(3) = 9$ (triangle with base 6 and height 3)
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $g(12) = 9$ (labeled), using the area of the triangular region from $x=6$ to $x=12$.

sample_responses.commentary: Linear segment forms a triangle; area $= \frac{1}{2}\cdot\text{base}\cdot\text{height} = \frac{1}{2}(6)(3)=9$.
Rubric requires a label; an unlabeled value earns no credit for either part (c) answer.

scoring_points.description: $g(0) = \int_6^0 f(t)\, dt = -\int_0^6 f(t)\, dt = -\frac{\pi(3)^2}{2} = -\frac{9\pi}{2}$ (semicircle of radius 3)

required_elements: $g(0) = \int_6^0 f(t)\, dt = -\int_0^6 f(t)\, dt = -\frac{\pi(3)^2}{2} = -\frac{9\pi}{2}$ (semicircle of radius 3)

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $g(0) = -\frac{9\pi}{2}$ (labeled), using the reversed-sign area of the upper semicircle of radius 3.

sample_responses.commentary: Reversing the limits introduces a negative sign; $\int_0^6 f(t)\,dt = $ area of upper semicircle $= \frac{\pi(3)^2}{2}$.
Integrating from $6$ to $0$ reverses the direction; the sign must be negative since the limits go from right to left over a positive-$f$ region.

parts.prompt: Find the value of $x$ at which $g$ attains an absolute minimum on the closed interval $-6 \le x \le 12$. Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Must consider $g'(x) = f(x) = 0$ (not just state $x = 0$). Must make a global argument comparing all candidate points: endpoints $x = -6$ and $x = 12$, and critical points $x = 0$ and $x = 6$. Alternate: $g'(x) \le 0$ for $-6 \le x < 0$ and $g'(x) \ge 0$ for $0 < x \le 12$, so absolute min at $x = 0$. Local argument does not earn P8.

scoring_points.description: Considers $g'(x) = f(x) = 0$: identifies critical points at $x = 0$ and $x = 6$

required_elements: Considers $g'(x) = f(x) = 0$: identifies critical points at $x = 0$

common_traps: Using a local argument instead of a global argument for optimization
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: considers $g'(x)=f(x)=0$ or discusses sign changes of $f$; identifies critical points $x=0$ and $x=6$.

sample_responses.response: Setting $g'(x) = f(x) = 0$: from the graph, $f(x)=0$ at $x=0$ and $x=6$ in $[-6,12]$.
sample_responses.commentary: Links $g'=f$ via FTC and reads the zeros of $f$ from the graph as critical points.

sample_responses.response: Just stating $x=0$ and $x=6$ without any setup.
sample_responses.commentary: Must consider $g'(x)=f(x)=0$ or equivalent; bare values do not earn P7.

scoring_points.description: Global justification: evaluates (or reasons about) $g$ at $x = -6$, $x = 0$, $x = 6$, and $x = 12$; $g(-6) = 0$, $g(0) = -\frac{9\pi}{2} \approx -14.14$, $g(6) = 0$, $g(12) = 9$

required_elements: Global justification: evaluates (or reasons about) $g$ at $x = -6$, $x = 0$, $x = 6$,

common_traps: Using a local argument instead of a global argument for optimization
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: global argument — evaluates or reasons about $g$ at all candidates: $g(-6)=0$, $g(0)=-\frac{9\pi}{2}$, $g(6)=0$, $g(12)=9$.

sample_responses.response: Candidates test: $g(-6)=0$, $g(0)=-\frac{9\pi}{2}\approx-14.1$, $g(6)=0$, $g(12)=9$. The minimum is $g(0)=-\frac{9\pi}{2}$, so absolute min at $x=0$.
sample_responses.commentary: Evaluates $g$ at both endpoints and all critical points; compares to identify global minimum.

sample_responses.response: $g''(0) = f'(0) > 0$, so $x=0$ is a local minimum.
sample_responses.commentary: Second derivative test is a local argument; for an absolute minimum on a closed interval, must evaluate $g$ at all candidate points globally.

common_traps: Using a local argument instead of a global argument for optimization

sample_responses.response: On $[-6,12]$, $g$ attains its absolute minimum at $x=0$.
sample_responses.commentary: $g(0)=-\frac{9\pi}{2}$ is less than all other candidate values.
